claimText: Calcium carbide reacts violently with seawater to produce highly flammable acetylene gas and is banned for fruit ripening in India under Food Safety and Standards Regulations, 2011.
evidenceExcerpt: Calcium carbide reacts violently with seawater to produce highly flammable acetylene gas and is banned for fruit ripening in India under FSSAI regulations.
examUse: Statement-based question on the chemical properties of hazardous substances and their regulatory status in India.
distractorHooks: Produces ethylene gas instead of acetylene
Is legally permitted for artificial fruit ripening in India
Reacts with seawater to produce non-flammable gases

claimText: The Indian Coast Guard is the nodal agency responsible for coordinating oil spill responses in India's maritime zones under the National Oil Spill Disaster Contingency Plan (1996).
evidenceExcerpt: The Indian Coast Guard acts as the nodal agency coordinating oil spill responses under the National Oil Spill Disaster Contingency Plan (1996).
examUse: Correct-pair or statement-based question identifying nodal agencies for specific disaster management frameworks.
distractorHooks: The Indian Navy is the nodal agency for oil spills
The National Disaster Response Force (NDRF) coordinates maritime oil spills
The Ministry of Earth Sciences is the nodal agency

claimText: The Merchant Shipping Act (1958) adopts the MARPOL conventions for maritime governance and marine pollution control in India.
evidenceExcerpt: The Merchant Shipping Act (1958) adopts MARPOL conventions for comprehensive maritime governance and pollution control in India.
examUse: Statement-based question on how international environmental conventions are implemented in Indian domestic law.
distractorHooks: The Environment Protection Act (1986) adopts MARPOL conventions
India has not adopted the MARPOL conventions into domestic law
The Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974 governs maritime shipping pollution

claimText: The BUNKER Convention (2001) holds shipowners strictly liable for pollution damage caused by bunker oil and mandates compulsory insurance coverage.
evidenceExcerpt: The BUNKER Convention (2001) holds shipowners liable for bunker oil pollution and mandates compulsory insurance coverage.
examUse: Correct-pair question matching international maritime conventions to their specific objectives and liability frameworks.
distractorHooks: Holds the flag state liable instead of the shipowner
Deals with the management of ballast water
Regulates the transboundary movement of hazardous wastes

claimText: Oil spills disrupt marine food chains by poisoning algae and negatively impacting benthic organisms such as clams and mussels.
evidenceExcerpt: Oil spills negatively impact benthic organisms like clams and mussels and poison algae, which disrupts marine food chains.
examUse: Statement-based question on the ecological consequences of marine pollution on specific marine trophic levels.
distractorHooks: Oil spills primarily benefit benthic organisms by providing carbon sources
Benthic organisms are immune to oil spills because they live at the ocean floor
Oil spills only affect pelagic fish and marine mammals
